title: Trump on lack of evacuation plan for Americans in Middle East: "It all happened very quickly"
author: CBS News
fullText: Okay, so you see the map here beneath me, the video here behind me. Day four of a war that President Trump says could go on for weeks. In and around Iran, tensions only grow more intense by the moment. Six US service members have now lost their lives in the Pentagon's Operation Epic Fury. We're also learning US forces hit one of Iran's underground nuclear facilities. US and Israeli missiles also hit several targets in the capital city of Tehran today. Uh, >> so first the explosion and then you see somebody moves to cover. Iran returning fire hitting the US embassy in Riad, Saudi Arabia. At this hour, the embassy is closed. People are urged to stay away. In fact, embassies in Beirut and Kuwait also ordered to shut down today. Now, for those reasons, the US State Department has a message to Americans who are living in 14 Middle Eastern countries. And here it is. Get out for your own safety. In fact, in a social media post, the State Department listed phone numbers to give Americans ways to evacuate. President Trump took questions from reporters today for the first time since the war began. He was asked about the chaos that travelers in the Middle East are facing. Listen, >> it happened all very quickly. We thought, and I thought maybe more so than most, I could ask Marco, but I thought we were going to have a situation where we were going to be attacked. They were getting ready to attack Israel. They were getting ready to attack others. You're seeing that right now. And a lot of those missiles that are heading in, those are stationary. Those were aimed there for a long period of time at these other countries. So, I think I was right about that. We attacked first. >> All right. So, let's go live now to Washington and CBS News White House reporter Aaron Naro. So, Aaron, the president says Iran was going to attack first, but what evidence is he providing on that point? Reed, no specific or concrete evidence has been provided by President Trump today, at least from that Oval Office appearance. But President Trump did say that he quote felt strongly that Iran was going to attack first given how negotiations between the US and Iran have been going, at least those indirect negotiations between the US and Iran to reach a nuclear deal. We know that for two for weeks now, the countries have been talking on some basis to get to that deal. And President Trump today said at one point, if anything, he might have forced is Israel's hand and not the other way around, but that the countries were prepared regardless for strikes. Now, this does run counter to what Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on Capitol Hill yesterday where he said that the Trump administration, the White House was aware that Israel was about to attack Iran, had known plans about that, and due to expected retaliation from Iran on US bases in the region, the US had to strike preemptively. We also know though that President Trump last December during a meeting with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in Mara Lago said that he would support Israeli strikes on Iran's ballistic missile program. So clearly coordination between the US and Israel on a strike predates the negotiations where Trump said he had a hunch that Iran was going to strike. >> Of course the next big and very important question is after this military campaign, who's going to lead in Iran? the president made some comment signaling what he thinks at least. What did you hear there? >> Yeah, I I think it was really notable when he was asked about uh Raza Palavi and the president seemed to put put some pour some cold water on him taking over and saying in the White House that he thinks someone in the country already should be the next leader. Take a listen to what President Trump told reporters earlier this afternoon. I guess the worst case would be we do this and then somebody takes over who's as bad as the previous person, right? That could happen. Uh we don't want that to happen. That would probably be the worst. You go through this and then uh in 5 years you realize you put somebody in who is no better. So we'd like to see somebody in there that's going to bring it back for the people and we'll see what happens with the people. You know, they have their chance. We've said don't do it yet. If you're going to go out and protest, don't do it yet. It's very dangerous out there. A lot of bombs are being dropped. But I would say that would be about the worst. >> Do you have someone in mind right now? Because you said all the people you did have in mind have been taken out. Will you >> Well, most of the people we had in mind are dead. So, you know, we had some in mind from that group that is uh is dead and now we have another group. They may be dead also. >> President, is he an option at all in your mind? >> I guess he is. Some people like them and we haven't been thinking about too much about that. It would seem to me that somebody from within might maybe would be more appropriate. >> Now, the strikes uh killing so many up to 50 uh leaders uh of the regime has provided a challenge for the administration as they look to who could potentially uh rule the country next. Though the administration, President Trump has made clear that they want someone quote a bit more moderate and not as radical as the former Supreme Leader. >> All right, Aaron Navaro live in Washington. And you know, just a moment ago, we were planning on going to Charlie Dageda, our CBS News correspondent in Tel Aviv. He's one of several of our correspondents fanned out there in the region. And just a moment ago, what we heard, you can see the live picture here, but we started to hear those sirens. those sirens that go off that warn people in the night to go into safe rooms. That has happened. That is why Charlie Dageda is not here in front of the camera because he is complying with those safety regulations. But we wanted to just pull that live picture up and sort of give you an idea of just how tense the situation is, not only in Iran, but for other people in the region, not the least of which in Tel Aviv, where once again we can tell you sirens just went off a moment ago. And that of course puts everybody on alert to go and take cover.
